attractions.tip: Enter through Southeast Gate 2 to avoid main crowds and get direct access to Anlan Suspension Bridge first thing in the morning when mist rises from the river for spectacular photos.
attractions.description: This entrance provides access to the ancient Dujiangyan irrigation system, a 2,000-year-old UNESCO World Heritage Site that still waters Chengdu's plains. Marvel at ingenious engineering that harmonizes with nature through elegant bamboo-and-stone structures spanning the roaring Min River.

attractions.name: Dujiangyan Panda Base
attractions.chinese: 成都大熊猫繁育研究基地都江堰繁育野放研究中心
attractions.tip: Arrive by 9am to catch feeding time when pandas are most active. The uphill walk can be steep, so wear comfortable shoes and bring water—there are fewer facilities here than at the main Chengdu base.
attractions.description: This hillside sanctuary offers a more intimate panda experience than Chengdu's main base, with fewer crowds and pandas in semi-wild enclosures that mimic their natural habitat. Watch these gentle giants climb trees and play in streams across expansive forested grounds.

attractions.name: Dujiangyan Shui Culture Square
attractions.tip: Visit in early evening when locals gather for tai chi and square dancing—it's the best time to see the plaza come alive and capture reflections of the surrounding mountains in the water features.
attractions.description: This riverside plaza celebrates the ancient hydraulic engineering heritage of Dujiangyan through water-themed sculptures and installations. A peaceful spot to appreciate the connection between the city's past and its life-giving irrigation system.

attractions.name: Tianfu Park
attractions.tip: Visit on weekday mornings for the best photo opportunities without crowds, especially around the central lake where locals practice tai chi against the backdrop of Chengdu's modern skyline.
attractions.description: This expansive modern park in Chengdu's Tianfu New Area features wide lawns, sculptural landscapes, and lake views that draw local families for weekend outings. The peaceful green spaces and contemporary design offer a refreshing escape from the city's historic temple gardens.

food.name: Mapo Tofu (Original)
food.where: Chen Mapo Tofu (陈麻婆豆腐) — Qingyang district, original since 1862
food.tip: The original, invented in Chengdu in 1862. Silky tofu in numbing chilli and minced pork sauce — the '麻' (numbing) must come from Sichuan pepper, not just chilli. Chen's is the original location. Order extra rice.

food.name: Dan Dan Noodles
food.where: Zhang Laoer Liangfen stalls or Jinli Street back lanes
food.tip: Thin noodles in sesame paste, chilli oil, minced pork and Sichuan pepper. The name comes from the shoulder poles (担) vendors carried. Best eaten standing at a street stall for the full experience.

food.name: Chengdu Hotpot
food.where: Piao Xiang Hotpot (飘香火锅) — local favourite since the 1990s
food.tip: Slightly different from Chongqing hotpot — more fragrant, less brutally numbing. The bone broth base is a Chengdu hallmark. Must-try: 鸭肠 (duck intestines) flash-cooked for exactly 7 seconds. Always order 毛肚 (tripe).

food.name: Kung Pao Chicken (Original)
food.where: Traditional Sichuan restaurants in Wuhou district
food.tip: The original Kung Pao Chicken is a Sichuan dish — nothing like the Westernised version. Diced chicken with dried chillies, peanuts and Sichuan pepper in a sweet-sour-spicy lychee-flavour sauce. Should be slightly smoky from wok hei.

food.name: Three Cannon Sticky Rice
food.where: Jinli Street back lanes — look for the theatrical stall
food.tip: Glutinous rice balls thrown onto a metal plate making a cannon sound (三大炮). Covered in sesame, red bean powder and brown sugar syrup. As theatrical as it is delicious.

food.name: Rabbit Head
food.where: Kuanzhai Alley area stalls and night markets
food.tip: Chengdu's most famous adventurous street food — whole rabbit head in spicy sauce. Split with chopsticks, eat the cheek meat and brain. Locals eat these like Singaporeans eat chicken wings. Very addictive, zero guilt.

food.name: Longchaoshou (Dragon Wonton)
food.where: Longchaoshou flagship — Chunxi Road, Jinjiang
food.tip: Chengdu's most iconic wonton shop, founded 1941. The wontons are silkier and thinner-skinned than northern versions. Original broth (清汤) or red oil (红油) dressing. The set meal with dandan noodles is the local classic order.

food.name: % Arabica Kuanzhai Alley
food.near: Kuanzhai Alley (Narrow Alley section), Qingyang District
food.must_order: Iced latte, pour-over, their signature % logo drink
food.tip: One of the most photographed Arabica branches in the world — B.L.U.E Architecture Studio converted a Qing dynasty siheyuan (courtyard home). The contrast of white minimalist interior against grey Sichuan tile roofs is stunning. Always a queue — go early morning for empty courtyard shots.

food.name: Heming Teahouse (People's Park)
food.chinese: 鹤鸣茶社 人民公园
food.must_order: Gaiwan jasmine tea — ¥15–20, refilled all day
food.tip: The most authentic Chengdu experience — over 100 years old, this teahouse inside People's Park is where locals spend entire mornings playing mahjong, having their ears cleaned, and drinking tea. Order a gaiwan of jasmine tea (¥15) and stay as long as you like. The earwax cleaning experience (¥15) is bizarre and wonderful.

food.name: Chengdu Sichuan Hot Pot (Haidilao)
food.must_order: Chengdu red oil broth, fresh vegetables, sliced beef, tripe
food.tip: Haidilao Chengdu has the best service of any hot pot chain — tableside noodle-pulling performance, free manicures while you wait, unlimited snacks. The Chengdu red oil broth is more complex and fragrant than other cities. Reserve online — always a queue. Kids are well looked after. The 'dancing noodles' show is genuine fun.

food.name: Horikoshi Coffee (锦里附近)
food.must_order: Japanese-style pour-over, matcha series
food.tip: Japanese-run specialty coffee near Jinli Street — meticulous brewing, beautiful minimalist space. Very different from the tourist food around Jinli. A peaceful respite after the busy ancient street. The Japanese owners speak some English and are very welcoming to foreign visitors.

food.name: Daci Temple Courtyard Cafe
food.near: Daci Temple, inside Taikoo Li, Jinjiang District
food.must_order: Traditional tea, creative Sichuan-flavoured coffee drinks
food.tip: The cafe inside the 1,600-year-old Daci Temple courtyard — one of Chengdu's most unique settings. The contrast of ancient Buddhist architecture and a modern specialty coffee bar is very Chengdu. Monks still practice here. The morning incense smoke drifting through the courtyard is extraordinary. Walk directly from Taikoo Li.

food.name: Panda Base Area Cafes
food.near: Chengdu Research Base of Giant Panda Breeding entrance
food.must_order: Panda-themed lattes and desserts
food.tip: Several panda-themed cafes cluster near the Panda Base entrance on Panda Avenue (熊猫大道). The panda latte art is extremely Instagrammable. Good for a coffee before or after the base visit. Arrive by 8am for the pandas — they are most active in the cool morning feeding hours.

food.name: Taikoo Li
food.tip: This is a shopping complex with many cafes inside. Head to the upper floors for quieter seating areas and better ambiance during weekday mornings.

food.name: Zuolan Coffee
food.tip: A hidden gem in Jinniu's northern area. Try their signature hand-drip coffee. Quiet atmosphere perfect for remote work or studying during weekday afternoons.

food.name: Friends Cafe
food.tip: A cozy neighborhood spot in Jinniu district perfect for remote work or catching up with friends. Try their specialty hand-drip coffee for an authentic local cafe experience.

food.name: Scandinavia
food.tip: Try their Nordic-style coffee and pastries for an authentic Scandinavian café experience. Arrives early on weekends as seating fills up quickly in this cozy neighborhood spot.

food.name: Maan Coffee
food.tip: Popular Korean-style cafe chain known for its spacious interiors and extensive menu. Try their signature waffles with coffee. Seats fill quickly on weekends, so visit during weekday afternoons.

food.name: The Temple Cafe
food.tip: Perfect spot to escape Chengdu's busy city centre. Visit mid-afternoon for a quieter atmosphere and try their specialty coffee blends alongside local pastries.

food.name: Mondoli
food.tip: Arrive early on weekends to snag a window seat. Their specialty coffee and house-made pastries are popular with locals. Quiet atmosphere perfect for working or reading.

food.name: Mid-lan Coffee
food.tip: A hidden gem in Pidu's western district. Try their signature hand-drip coffee for a quieter alternative to crowded downtown cafes. Ideal for afternoon work sessions.

food.name: Foooo
food.tip: A hidden gem in Pidu's west side. Perfect for a quiet afternoon away from tourist crowds. Arrive early on weekends to secure a good seat.

food.name: Luckin Coffee No.1024
food.tip: Order the Raw Coconut Latte or seasonal fruit-based drinks for best value. Use the app for frequent promotional discounts and buy-one-get-one deals during morning hours.

food.name: Paradise Island Cafe
food.tip: Popular with locals in Pidu district. Try their specialty coffee blends and pastries. Weekday afternoons are quieter for a relaxing break from the city.

food.name: Qinshanzhai
food.tip: Popular stop for travelers visiting nearby Sanxingdui Museum. Arrive before noon to avoid tour group rush. Try local Sichuan farmhouse-style dishes.

food.name: Heming Tea House
food.tip: Visit in the morning for the authentic local atmosphere with ear cleaning services and mahjong players. Try their covered bowl tea and the signature three-cannon rice cake snack.

food.name: Song Cafe
food.tip: Perfect pit stop when visiting Sanxingdui Museum. Arrive early morning for quieter atmosphere and fresh pastries before the tour groups descend.

food.name: Jinjiang Bashu Weiyuan
food.tip: Classic Sichuan restaurant known for authentic local flavors. Try the mapo tofu and twice-cooked pork. Arrive before noon to avoid peak lunch crowds.

food.name: Fengya Old Tree Coffee Dujiangyan
food.chinese: 风雅老树咖啡都江堰店
food.tip: Perfect rest stop after exploring Dujiangyan Irrigation System. Grab a seat by the window for views of old town streets while enjoying their signature hand-drip coffee.

food.name: Sandglass Coffee
food.tip: Perfect rest stop after visiting Dujiangyan Irrigation System. Try their hand-drip coffee and grab a window seat to watch local life unfold while recharging.

food.name: A Cup for Lola
food.tip: Hidden gem with excellent specialty coffee. Try their signature hand-drip brews. Visit on weekday mornings to avoid crowds and enjoy the peaceful atmosphere.

food.name: Tiantian Restaurant
food.tip: A local neighborhood spot in Tianfu South area. Best for casual Sichuan home-style dishes. Visit during lunch hours for fresher ingredients and faster service.

food.name: Chen Mapo Tofu Restaurant
food.tip: Order the signature mapo tofu with extra numbing Sichuan peppercorns. Arrive before noon to avoid crowds. Pair with plain rice to balance the intense spice.

food.name: Angel Cafe
food.tip: A hidden gem in Tianfu South with perfect 5-star ratings. Arrive early on weekends as seating can be limited in this cozy neighborhood spot.

food.name: Caffe Bene
food.tip: This Korean coffee chain offers waffles and bingsu alongside standard coffee drinks. Visit during off-peak afternoon hours for a quieter atmosphere and faster service.

hotels.name: The Temple House Chengdu
hotels.tip: Chengdu's most celebrated hotel — inside Taikoo Li next to the ancient Daci Temple. Courtyard design blending Sichuan heritage with modern luxury. The best restaurant for Sichuan cuisine. Walk to Chunxi Road. Staff warmth consistently top-rated. A genuine 'sense of place' hotel.

hotels.name: Ritz-Carlton Chengdu
hotels.chinese: 成都富力丽思卡尔顿酒店
hotels.tip: Polished Ritz-Carlton in a convenient downtown location — great pool, jacuzzi and sauna. Diptyque amenities, TWG tea, Michelin-quality dining nearby. Consistently praised for warm, attentive service. Walk to Kuanzhai Alley in 15 minutes.

hotels.name: Dorsett Chengdu
hotels.tip: Excellent mid-range in the Chunxi Road heart — spacious rooms, good for families (kids get panda-themed gifts if you skip daily cleaning). Outdoor Zen-garden courtyard. Metro access. Praised for helpful, English-friendly staff. Great value compared to nearby 5-stars.

hotels.name: Buddha Zen Hotel Chengdu
hotels.tip: Beautifully designed courtyard hotel in a renovated heritage building next to Wenshu Monastery — peaceful Zen aesthetic, wooden furnishings, tea room on-site. 5-min walk to Wenshuyuan metro. Walk to Kuanzhai Alley. A very different feel from the chain hotels — calm and characterful.

hotels.name: Ibis Chengdu Chunxi
hotels.tip: Unbeatable budget location — Chunxi Road metro station at the door, Taikoo Li 3 minutes' walk. Accor Ibis reliability. Small but clean rooms. Great for travellers spending most time out exploring. Best budget location in all of Chengdu.

chengdu.recommended_base_reason: Close to Panda Base, walkable to Kuanzhai Alley and People's Park

surrounds.name: Leshan Giant Buddha
surrounds.tip: World's largest stone Buddha — 71m tall, carved into a cliff at the junction of 3 rivers (Minjiang, Dadu, Qingyi). Been gazing at boats since 713 AD. TWO ways to see it: (1) boat cruise (¥70 extra) looking up from water — most dramatic, the sheer scale hits you, (2) the 九曲栈道 (Nine Bends Ancient Path) walking down beside the Buddha — steep zigzag steps alongside the full body. Do the boat first, then the stairs if energy allows. The Buddha's head is level with the clifftop — walk out and look DOWN at it from the top. Kids love comparing their size to the Buddha's toenails (wider than a person is tall). Book tickets 3 days ahead — limited daily quota.

surrounds.name: Mount Qingcheng (Front Mountain)
surrounds.tip: One of China's most sacred Taoist mountains — 'Qingcheng is the most serene mountain in the world.' Ancient Taoist temples and shrines scattered throughout dense bamboo forest. Front mountain has the cultural sites (Tianshi Cave, Supreme Purity Palace). Take the cable car up (¥35), walk down. The boat across the lake to the cable car adds a scenic opener. Not suitable for toddlers. Combine with Dujiangyan on same day (15 min drive). Avoid summer weekends — very crowded.

surrounds.name: Dujiangyan Irrigation System
surrounds.tip: The world's oldest and only surviving non-dam hydraulic engineering system — built in 256 BC by Li Bing, still irrigating 5.3 million hectares today. UNESCO World Heritage. Walk across Anlan Suspension Bridge over the Minjiang River for the best views of the fish-mouth diversion. The evening sound and light show '上善都江堰' (¥85 extra) uses drones and projections to tell the story of the irrigation system — one of China's best cultural performances. Kids cross the swaying suspension bridge and learn about ancient engineering. Same day as Qingcheng.

surrounds.name: Dujiangyan Panda Base (Volunteer Experience)
surrounds.chinese: 都江堰熊猫基地 — 公益志愿者
surrounds.tip: The panda keeper for a day experience (8+): feed pandas bamboo, clean enclosures, make enrichment toys. Limited to small groups. Much more intimate than the main Chengdu base. ¥1,500/person is expensive but for genuine panda lovers it's extraordinary. Book 2+ weeks ahead through official channels — fills up fast. Combine with Dujiangyan water system in the afternoon. Even without the volunteer programme, this base as a regular visit (¥150) is excellent — fewer crowds than Chengdu.

surrounds.name: Mount Emei (Emeishan)
surrounds.seasonal_note: Winter (Nov–Feb) has snow and rime ice at summit — magical but cold. Summer clouds often obscure summit views. Oct is peak for golden monkey sightings.
surrounds.tip: China's highest Buddhist sacred mountain — 3,099m. Summit Golden Temple glitters gold against cloud sea. The cable car to the top (¥30 each way) saves 8hrs of hiking — essential for most visitors. BEWARE: Emei monkeys are aggressive — don't carry visible food or snacks, don't make eye contact, don't show phones or cameras carelessly. They WILL steal. The 'sea of clouds' below the summit on clear mornings is extraordinary. Most visitors combine with Leshan for a 2-day overnight trip. Full summit access needs 2 days.

surrounds.name: Sanxingdui Bronze Age Museum
surrounds.tip: One of the most extraordinary archaeological discoveries in history — a 3,000-year-old Bronze Age civilisation with no connection to known Chinese history. The giant bronze masks with protruding eyes, the 4m tall bronze tree, the gold sceptre — all completely unlike anything else from this period. Became viral on Chinese social media ('China's aliens'). Two museums — new (2023) and old. New exhibits from 2021–2024 ongoing excavations are mind-blowing. Kids are fascinated by the 'alien-looking' artefacts. Essential if you have a history-curious traveller in the group.

surrounds.name: Xiling Snow Mountain
surrounds.seasonal_note: Snow season December to March. Not as good as Changbaishan but the nearest ski resort to Chengdu — 1.5hr drive.
surrounds.tip: Chengdu's closest ski resort — basic slopes but convenient for first-time skiers or families who don't want to travel to Northeast China. Beginner-friendly with ski instructors. Also good for summer hiking and cable car scenic views. Snow quality varies — check conditions before going. Popular with Chengdu families in winter holidays.

chengdu.surrounds_note: Day trips from Chengdu — Leshan, Qingcheng, Dujiangyan, Emei, Sanxingdui

kids_attractions.name: Chengdu Research Base of Giant Panda Breeding
kids_attractions.chinese: 成都大熊猫繁育研究基地
kids_attractions.tip: The world's best place to see giant pandas — 80+ pandas on site. GO AT 8AM when pandas are active and feeding. By 10am they're napping and barely visible. The baby panda nursery (seasonal) is extraordinary. Don't book the 'panda keeper for a day' experience online — it's overpriced for what it is. Free strollers available.

kids_attractions.name: Chengdu HHAn Polar Ocean World
kids_attractions.tip: Southwest China's largest marine park — polar bears, sea lions, sharks, penguins, belugas. Daily whale and dolphin shows, wave pool and children's water play area. The polar bear habitat viewing is the highlight. A good rainy-day option. Kids under 1m enter free.

kids_attractions.name: Global Center Indoor Water Park (Paradise Island)
kids_attractions.tip: Inside the world's largest building — a massive indoor waterpark with wave pools, water slides and a synthetic beach. Year-round regardless of weather. The lazy river and toddler play area are excellent for under-5s. The surfing simulator is popular with older kids. Combine with a visit to the Global Center shopping mall.

kids_attractions.name: Dujiangyan Panda Base (Volunteer Experience)
kids_attractions.tip: The best place for the panda keeper experience (ages 8+ only, ¥1,500/person) — feed pandas, clean their enclosures, make bamboo baskets. Far more personal than the main Chengdu base. Also excellent just as a standard visit — fewer crowds than the city base. Combine with the nearby Dujiangyan irrigation system (UNESCO).

beijing.name: Beijing
beijing.tagline: 5,000 Years of History in One City

attractions.name: Forbidden City (Palace Museum)
attractions.tip: 980 buildings, 8,700 rooms, 600 years. Book weeks ahead — only sold online, no walk-in. Arrive at 8:30am opening. Skip the front courtyard crowds and go directly to the Treasure Hall and Clock Museum (¥10 each extra). The Imperial Garden at the back is often overlooked.
attractions.description: Ninety-eight-year-old Ming dynasty palace complex where 24 emperors ruled for 500 years — the world's largest preserved collection of ancient wooden structures. Walk through the Meridian Gate at dawn when golden light floods the 9,000 rooms and the crowds haven't yet arrived.

attractions.name: Great Wall (Mutianyu Section)
attractions.tip: Skip crowded Badaling — Mutianyu has 70% forest coverage and far fewer tourists. Take the cable car up (¥100 return). The toboggan ride back down is brilliant. Most towers are accessible. Hire a driver from Beijing (¥400 return) or book the tourist bus.
attractions.description: The most dramatic section of the world's greatest engineering feat — 2,300 years old, snaking across mountain ridges through 70% forested landscape far from the tourist chaos of Badaling. Take the toboggan run down for the most exhilarating exit from any UNESCO site.
attractions.photo_attribution: Mutianyu Great Wall Scenic Area Ticket Office

attractions.name: Temple of Heaven
attractions.tip: Go at 7am — the park opens before the temple buildings. You'll see elderly locals doing tai chi, playing Chinese chess and performing Peking opera in the echoey walls. The Hall of Prayer for Good Harvests has extraordinary triple-roof geometry. Blue tiles represent heaven.
attractions.description: A Ming dynasty masterpiece where emperors once performed sacred rituals to ensure good harvests — the circular Hall of Prayer for Good Harvests is the most perfect example of Chinese imperial architecture. Arrive at 7am to watch elderly Beijingers practise tai chi, play erhu and fly kites in the surrounding park.

attractions.name: Summer Palace
attractions.tip: Imperial garden with 3,000+ structures around Kunming Lake. Rent a rowboat (¥50) — views of the marble boat from the water are extraordinary. The 728m Long Corridor (长廊) has 14,000 paintings. Autumn colours are spectacular. Avoid Golden Week.
attractions.description: China's most beautiful imperial garden — 3km² of lakes, pavilions, covered walkways and rockery gardens where the Qing dynasty court retreated from Beijing's summer heat. The 728m Long Corridor painted with 14,000 scenes from Chinese history and mythology is extraordinary at any pace.

attractions.name: Nanluoguxiang Hutong
attractions.tip: Skip the main alley — duck into the 8 side hutongs (try Mao'er Hutong and Banchang Hutong) for authentic old Beijing. Get the famous clay-pot yoghurt (¥5) and sour plum juice. Evening is most atmospheric when lanterns light up.
attractions.description: Beijing's most atmospheric surviving hutong alleyway — 786 metres of Ming-era grey-brick lanes lined with indie cafes, vinyl record shops and centuries-old courtyard homes. The best time is early morning when locals fetch breakfast and the tourist crowds are still asleep.

attractions.name: Tiananmen Square & Gate
attractions.tip: Bring your passport — required to enter the square. The flag-raising ceremony at sunrise draws huge crowds but is genuinely moving. The Tiananmen Gate tower can be climbed (¥15) for a face-to-face view of the famous portrait. Free after 5pm.
attractions.description: A must-visit landmark in Beijing that showcases the best of local culture and history. Ask your hotel concierge for the best time to visit and any advance booking requirements.

attractions.name: Yonghe Lama Temple
attractions.tip: China's most important Tibetan Buddhist temple. The 26m sandalwood Maitreya Buddha carved from a single tree trunk is breathtaking. Incense smoke fills the courtyards at morning prayer time. In snow, this rivals the Forbidden City without the crowds.
attractions.description: A must-visit temple in Beijing that showcases the best of local culture and history. Ask your hotel concierge for the best time to visit and any advance booking requirements.

attractions.name: 798 Art District
attractions.tip: Former Bauhaus-style military factory complex turned contemporary art hub. Free to roam. UCCA gallery (¥60) has world-class exhibitions. The 'Tank' building has dramatic permanent installations. Best on weekday afternoons. Weekend crowds are significant.
attractions.description: A Bauhaus factory complex from the 1950s transformed into Beijing's most vibrant contemporary art hub — hundreds of galleries, installation spaces and creative studios inside buildings still bearing Maoist revolutionary slogans. The contrast of socialist industrial architecture with cutting-edge contemporary art is genuinely thrilling.

attractions.name: Jingshan Park (Panoramic View)
attractions.tip: Only ¥2 and the absolute best view of the Forbidden City from above. 10 minutes walk from the palace's north gate. Sunrise from the Wanchun Pavilion is extraordinary — arrive by 5:30am in summer. Elderly locals sing Peking opera in the park below.
attractions.description: A man-made hill created from the earth excavated when the Forbidden City's moat was dug — climb to the five-pavilion summit for the single best panoramic view of the entire Forbidden City laid out below you. Every Beijing photographer's favourite shot, best at dawn or sunset.
attractions.photo_query: jingshan park beijing forbidden city view

attractions.name: National Museum of China
attractions.tip: One of the world's largest museums — 1.4M artefacts, free entry, but must book via WeChat. The 'Ancient China' permanent exhibition spanning 5,000 years is unmissable. The bronze collection is world-class. Allow 3+ hours minimum. Closed Mondays.
attractions.description: A must-visit museum in Beijing that showcases the best of local culture and history. Ask your hotel concierge for the best time to visit and any advance booking requirements.

attractions.name: Houhai Lake & Hutong District
attractions.tip: Beautiful lake surrounded by hutongs, bars and traditional courtyard restaurants. Rent a pedal boat in summer (¥40/hr). In winter the lake freezes and locals ice skate. Stroll the hutong streets after dark — the bar scene here is low-key and local.
attractions.description: A must-visit local-hangout in Beijing that showcases the best of local culture and history. Ask your hotel concierge for the best time to visit and any advance booking requirements.

attractions.name: Temple of Earth (Ditan Park)
attractions.tip: The square counterpart to the round Temple of Heaven — where emperors worshipped the Earth. Far less visited than its famous sibling. The silver ginkgo trees in November turn the park gold — one of Beijing's most beautiful seasonal spectacles. Free entry.
attractions.description: A must-visit historic-site in Beijing that showcases the best of local culture and history. Ask your hotel concierge for the best time to visit and any advance booking requirements.

attractions.name: Wangfujing Street & Snack Alley
attractions.tip: Beijing's most famous commercial street. The snack alley side street has scorpions, starfish and grasshoppers on sticks — mostly tourist theatre, but fun. For serious shopping: SKP department store at the south end is better. Best in the evening when lit up.
attractions.description: Beijing's most famous shopping thoroughfare where centuries of commerce meet modern luxury — the underground archaeological site beneath the street reveals 20,000-year-old human remains beneath your feet. The snack alley running parallel is where you'll find scorpion skewers, candied hawthorn and old-school Beijing street food.

attractions.name: Five Daos Alley (Wudaoying Hutong)
attractions.tip: Next to Lama Temple — a quiet hutong with excellent independent cafes, craft beer bars and vintage shops. Less touristy than Nanluoguxiang but more interesting. The rooftops of some bars overlook the temple roofs. Great for a slow afternoon.
attractions.description: A must-visit local-hangout in Beijing that showcases the best of local culture and history. Ask your hotel concierge for the best time to visit and any advance booking requirements.

attractions.name: Drum Tower & Bell Tower
attractions.tip: Two 14th-century towers that kept time for the city for 600 years. Drum beating performance every 30 minutes. Climb the Drum Tower for great views over the surrounding hutong rooftops. Combine with Houhai Lake and Nanluoguxiang nearby for a full day.
attractions.description: Twin Tang dynasty towers that once marked the heartbeat of old Beijing — the Drum Tower's 25 drums were struck at dusk to close the city gates, the Bell Tower's bronze bell rang at dawn to open them. Climb to the top for the best rooftop view of the hutong sea spreading in every direction.

attractions.name: Old Summer Palace (Yuanmingyuan)
attractions.tip: The ruins of the original Summer Palace — deliberately left unrestored after being looted and burned by British-French forces in 1860. Hauntingly beautiful and historically significant. The lotus lake in summer is extraordinary. Far fewer visitors than New Summer Palace.
attractions.description: China's most beautiful imperial garden — 3km² of lakes, pavilions, covered walkways and rockery gardens where the Qing dynasty court retreated from Beijing's summer heat. The 728m Long Corridor painted with 14,000 scenes from Chinese history and mythology is extraordinary at any pace.

attractions.name: Badachu (Eight Great Sites)
attractions.tip: Eight ancient temples along a forested hillside in western Beijing — almost completely unknown to tourists. Autumn maple leaves and spring cherry blossoms are spectacular. The Buddhist relics here predate some Forbidden City artefacts. ¥10 entry. Take the cable car up.
attractions.description: A must-visit nature in Beijing that showcases the best of local culture and history. Ask your hotel concierge for the best time to visit and any advance booking requirements.

attractions.name: Niujie Mosque & Halal Food Street
attractions.tip: Beijing's oldest mosque (996 AD) in the city's Muslim Quarter. The most reliable halal food area in Beijing. Juyibao Yuan lamb hotpot (聚宝源) is the city's best — get there at opening. The street has Beijing's best Muslim pastries and sweets.
attractions.description: Beijing's oldest mosque — dating from 996AD, designed in Chinese architectural style with Arabic calligraphy instead of Buddha statues. The surrounding Niujie neighbourhood is Beijing's Hui Muslim heart, lined with halal lamb skewers, fried dough rings and sesame flatbreads that have been sold on these streets for a thousand years.

attractions.name: Beijing Zoo & Panda House
attractions.tip: One of the best value attractions in Beijing — ¥20 for one of China's best zoos. The separate panda house is ¥5 extra. Unlike Chengdu's base, you can get remarkably close to the pandas here. The ocean aquarium inside the zoo is excellent for families.
attractions.description: A must-visit attraction in Beijing that showcases the best of local culture and history. Ask your hotel concierge for the best time to visit and any advance booking requirements.

attractions.name: Sanlitun & Workers' Stadium
attractions.tip: Beijing's trendiest neighbourhood. Workers' Stadium was renovated in 2023 into a world-class multi-use venue. The 'Village' shopping is good but expensive. The real draw is wandering the streets at night — international restaurants, rooftop bars and the buzz of the city's young crowd.
attractions.description: Beijing's most international neighbourhood — the newly rebuilt Workers' Stadium complex houses world-class music venues and the city's most sophisticated dining scene alongside the famous Sanlitun Village shopping area. At night this is where Beijing's young professional class and expat community converge.

attractions.name: Fragrant Hills (Xiangshan Park)
attractions.tip: Best in late October when the maple leaves turn crimson — the autumn colour show here is Beijing's most spectacular seasonal event. Take the cable car up (¥50) and walk down through the red maple groves. Get there before 8am to beat the leaf-peeping crowds.
attractions.description: A former imperial hunting ground turned spectacular autumn colour destination — October transforms the hillsides into a blaze of red, orange and gold as thousands of smoke trees turn simultaneously. Climb to the glazed-tile octagonal pagoda at the summit for views over the entire Beijing plain.

attractions.name: Shichahai Historic District
attractions.tip: Three connected lakes in the centre of Beijing's oldest neighbourhood. Less touristy than nearby Houhai, more authentic. Rent a bicycle and circle all three lakes (6km). The Silver Ingot Bridge (银锭桥) gives rare views of the Western Hills on a clear day.
attractions.description: A must-visit local-hangout in Beijing that showcases the best of local culture and history. Ask your hotel concierge for the best time to visit and any advance booking requirements.

attractions.name: Forbidden City
attractions.tip: Enter through the less crowded west gate (Xihua Gate) and visit the tranquil Palace of Tranquil Longevity in the northeast corner which most tour groups skip. Buy tickets online days in advance as daily visitor numbers are capped.
attractions.description: The world's largest imperial palace complex sprawls across 180 acres with vermillion walls, golden roofs, and 980 buildings that housed 24 emperors across five centuries. Walk through the same ceremonial halls and private chambers where China's Ming and Qing dynasties ruled, now preserving priceless imperial treasures.

attractions.name: Prince Gong Mansion
attractions.tip: Visit on weekday mornings to avoid tour groups. The garden's back section near the rockery and opera stage is often overlooked but offers the most photogenic corners and peaceful atmosphere.
attractions.description: This sprawling 1777 estate showcases the opulent lifestyle of Qing Dynasty royalty through ornate halls, tranquil courtyards, and a classical Chinese garden. The highlight is the hidden 'Fu' (fortune) stele carved by Emperor Kangxi, believed to bring good luck to those who touch it.

attractions.name: Great Wall of Badaling
attractions.tip: Arrive right at opening (7:30 AM) or after 3 PM to avoid crushing tour groups. The left route (north) is steeper but less crowded than the right, with better photo opportunities at the eighth watchtower.
attractions.description: The most accessible section of the Great Wall ascends dramatically to 3,282 feet through mountain peaks, its Ming Dynasty watchtowers commanding sweeping views. This is where the iconic serpentine wall photos come to life, offering the quintessential Great Wall experience within reach of Beijing.

attractions.name: Temple of Heaven Park Children's Paradise
attractions.tip: Visit on weekday mornings when it's less crowded and local grandparents bring children, creating an authentic glimpse into Beijing family life while kids play.
attractions.description: A charming playground tucked within the historic Temple of Heaven grounds, offering swings, slides, and climbing frames under ancient trees. Perfect for families needing a playful break between exploring Beijing's imperial heritage sites.

attractions.name: Beijing Drum Tower and Bell Tower
attractions.tip: Visit at 9:30am or 3:30pm for the drum performance, then immediately walk to the Bell Tower across the square—your Drum Tower ticket gives a discount on Bell Tower admission.
attractions.description: These twin Ming dynasty towers stand as Beijing's ancient timekeepers, where massive drums and bells once regulated the rhythm of imperial life. Climb the steep wooden stairs for rooftop views over the surrounding hutongs and watch traditional drum performances that echo through the historic neighborhood.

attractions.name: Beijing Bell and Drum Towers
attractions.tip: Visit at 9:30am or 11:30am to catch the drum performance on the upper level. The climb up the Drum Tower is steep with 69 steps, but the Bell Tower's 63 steps are even steeper, so pace yourself.
attractions.description: Two imposing Ming Dynasty towers once marked time for the entire imperial capital, standing like sentinels over Beijing's ancient hutong neighborhoods. Climb the steep wooden stairs for panoramic rooftop views and witness traditional drum performances that echo across the old city.

attractions.name: Bell Tower and Drum Tower Culture Square
attractions.tip: Visit at dusk when both towers are illuminated and the square fills with locals dancing and socializing. The surrounding hutong alleyways have better photo angles of both towers than the square itself.
attractions.description: This lively public square sits between Beijing's ancient Bell and Drum Towers, where timekeeping instruments once regulated imperial life. The open plaza offers unobstructed views of both Ming Dynasty structures while locals practice tai chi, fly kites, and gather for traditional performances.

attractions.name: Beijing Olympic Park
attractions.tip: Visit after sunset when the Bird's Nest and Water Cube are illuminated with colorful LED displays. Enter from the south gate for the best progressive view of both stadiums.
attractions.description: This sprawling public park showcases Beijing's 2008 Olympic legacy through iconic structures like the Bird's Nest stadium and Water Cube, connected by tree-lined boulevards and modern sculptures. The dramatic contemporary architecture against wide-open spaces creates a striking contrast to Beijing's ancient sites.

attractions.name: National Stadium (Bird's Nest)
attractions.tip: Visit at dusk when the structure is illuminated with changing LED lights—the golden hour glow through the steel beams creates stunning photography without the daytime crowds and harsh shadows.
attractions.description: This striking steel lattice stadium resembles a giant bird's nest and remains Beijing's most recognizable Olympic legacy from 2008. Walk around its dramatic exterior or venture inside to explore the sports venue that hosted the world's most watched opening ceremony.

attractions.name: Beihai Park
attractions.tip: Rent a paddle boat on the lake for the best views of the White Dagoba and avoid weekends when it gets crowded with local families.
attractions.description: This 69-hectare imperial garden surrounds a serene lake dominated by a white dagoba perched atop Jade Island, dating back nearly 1,000 years. The park offers a tranquil escape with classical pavilions, ancient trees, and locals practicing tai chi at dawn.

attractions.name: Beijing Aquarium
attractions.tip: Visit on weekday mornings to avoid overwhelming crowds, especially during the dolphin show. The performance seats fill up 20 minutes early, so arrive at the theater entrance well before showtime to secure good viewing spots.
attractions.description: One of Asia's largest inland aquariums showcases thousands of marine species from coral reef fish to Arctic belugas across themed exhibition halls. The highlight is the impressive marine mammal performance featuring dolphins, sea lions, and whales in a state-of-the-art indoor theater.

attractions.name: Blue Zoo Beijing
attractions.tip: Visit on weekday mornings to avoid crowds, especially during school holidays. The shark feeding show at 11am offers the best photo opportunities through the underwater tunnel.
attractions.description: This compact aquarium in the Xicheng district showcases underwater ecosystems through glass tunnels and themed zones. Popular with local families for its interactive touch pools and scheduled feeding shows featuring rays and sharks.

food.name: Peking Duck
food.where: Da Dong (大董) — creative version; Quanjude (全聚德) Qianmen — classic since 1864
food.tip: The definitive Beijing experience. Order one duck between 2 people. Eat the crispy skin first with just sugar — this is how connoisseurs eat it. Da Dong's version has thinner, crispier skin than Quanjude. Must pre-book Da Dong 1–2 days ahead.

food.name: Zha Jiang Mian (Beijing Noodles)
food.where: Old Beijing Noodle King (老北京炸酱面大王) near Qianmen
food.tip: Beijing's signature noodle — thick wheat noodles topped with fried soybean paste and minced pork, garnished with 8 fresh vegetable toppings you mix yourself. A Ming dynasty institution. The texture should be silky from rendered lard.

food.name: Lamb Hotpot (Halal)
food.where: Juyibao Yuan (聚宝源) on Niujie — halal certified
food.tip: Beijing's classic Mongolian copper pot hotpot. Paper-thin lamb slices in clear broth, dipped in sesame paste. Juyibao Yuan on Niujie (牛街) is halal-certified and considered the city's best. Always packed — arrive at opening or book ahead.

food.name: Jian Bing (Street Crepe)
food.where: Street carts at metro exits citywide — especially in the morning
food.tip: Beijing's legendary breakfast crepe. Thin mung bean batter on a griddle, topped with egg, spring onion, chilli sauce, fermented bean paste and a crispy wonton wafer. Only ¥10. Always made to order in under 2 minutes. The most democratic food in Beijing.

food.name: Tanghulu (Sugar-Coated Haws)
food.where: Wangfujing Snack Street and hutong areas
food.tip: Crunchy sugar-coated hawthorn berries on a stick — Beijing's most iconic snack since the Song dynasty. The traditional hawthorn-only version is best. Best in winter when the sugar shell crackles in the cold. Modern versions with strawberry or kiwi are everywhere.

food.name: Douzhi (Fermented Mung Bean Drink)
food.where: Traditional breakfast stalls in old hutong areas
food.tip: The most divisive food in Beijing — fermented mung bean juice with a sour, funky smell. Old Beijingers swear by it for breakfast with fried dough rings (焦圈) and pickled vegetables. Considered a rite of passage. You either love it or flee the stall. Worth trying once.

food.name: Jiao Quan (Fried Dough Rings)
food.where: Traditional breakfast stalls throughout the city
food.tip: Small, perfectly golden deep-fried dough rings — the Beijing companion to douzhi and porridge. Lighter and crispier than you'd expect. The old hutong breakfast shops that serve these are disappearing — seek them out in Drum Tower and Niujie areas.

food.name: Metal Hands Cafe
food.near: Nanluoguxiang hutong, 5 min walk from Drum Tower
food.must_order: Pistachio Dirty, Vienna Coffee
food.tip: Only Asian cafe in global top 50. French retro-industrial style inside a historic hutong courtyard. The bar counter lets you watch the baristas up close. Expect a queue on weekends.

food.name: Voyage Coffee
food.must_order: Single-origin pour-over, seasonal filter coffee
food.tip: Tucked inside a 100-year-old courtyard at the Yuhe Heritage Site. Two interconnected courtyards — the back one has shady trees and riverside seating. Magnolia blooms in spring (Mar–Apr), golden ginkgo in autumn (Oct–Nov). Stunning in both seasons.

food.name: Fú Dǐng Café
food.must_order: Matcha latte, pour-over with temple view
food.tip: Rooftop terrace overlooking the Lama Temple rooftops — one of Beijing's best cafe views. Zen-inspired interior combining Chinese aesthetics with Western coffee. Best at golden hour (4–6pm) when the temple tiles glow.

food.name: Wudaoying Hutong Cafes
food.must_order: Whatever looks interesting — browse first
food.tip: A quieter alternative to Nanluoguxiang — 500m hutong lined with independent cafes, bookshops and art studios. Much less crowded, more authentic atmosphere. The entire street is photogenic. Go on weekday mornings for an empty hutong experience.

food.name: Peking Duck at Siheyuan
food.must_order: Full Peking duck set — pancakes, hoisin, scallion
food.tip: TRB Hutong is the best sit-down Peking duck experience in the hutong area — converted courtyard, exceptional duck. For budget Peking duck, Da Dong (大董) near Dongsi is the most celebrated affordable version. Both require booking ahead.

food.name: Palace Museum Café (Forbidden City)
food.must_order: Palace-themed drinks — osmanthus latte, chrysanthemum tea latte
food.tip: Multiple cafes inside the palace grounds — look for ones in the side courtyards. The combination of Ming dynasty architecture and your coffee cup makes for extraordinary photos. Only accessible with Forbidden City entry ticket.

food.name: Wangfujing Snack Street
food.near: Wangfujing, 5 min walk from Forbidden City east gate
food.must_order: Tanghulu (candied hawthorn), fried scorpion (for photos), jianbing
food.tip: Classic Beijing street food experience. The fried scorpions and starfish are mostly for tourist photos — the ones actually worth eating are tanghulu (glazed hawthorn on a stick), jianbing (savoury crepe) and candied haw flakes. Go at dusk when it lights up.

food.name: Still Water Coffee
food.near: Shichahai / Xicheng area, 20 min walk from Forbidden City
food.must_order: Orange Iced Americano, Pour-over Coffee
food.tip: A bamboo-and-pond courtyard cafe in Xicheng — wooden doors, flowing water, bamboo grove. You receive a horse-shaped buzzer after ordering which is great for photos. The upstairs terrace overlooks grey Beijing rooftops. One of Beijing's most calming cafes.

food.name: 798 Art District Cafes
food.near: 798 Art District, 15 min from Olympic Park
food.must_order: House espresso, seasonal pastries
food.tip: Dozens of excellent cafes inside the converted Bauhaus factory complex. UCCA Cafe (inside UCCA art gallery) is the most acclaimed. The industrial architecture with exposed Mao-era slogans and contemporary art is extraordinary. Best on weekday mornings before tour groups arrive.

food.name: Olympic Park Night Walk + Street Food
food.must_order: Grilled lamb skewers, cold noodles, local snacks
food.tip: The area around the Bird's Nest and Water Cube comes alive at night with street food vendors and illuminated Olympic structures. Walk the lake promenade with food and take photos of the lit-up stadiums reflected in the water. Best 7–10pm.

food.name: Houhai Lakeside Bars & Cafes
food.must_order: Coffee by day, local beer or cocktails by evening
food.tip: The waterfront cafes around Houhai Lake are a Beijing institution. Cycle around the lake then stop for coffee at a lakeside terrace. At night it transforms into a bar scene. The reflection of traditional architecture in the lake is beautiful at dusk.

food.name: Summer Palace Teahouse
food.must_order: Jasmine tea, lotus seed pastries
food.tip: Traditional teahouse inside the Summer Palace grounds with lakeside pavilion setting. The combination of imperial garden views and a pot of Chinese tea is a quintessential Beijing experience. Included with Summer Palace admission. Best in the late afternoon.

food.name: Dongcheng Furong Restaurant
food.tip: Popular lunch spot for tourists visiting the Forbidden City. Arrive before noon to avoid peak crowds. Try their Beijing home-style dishes at reasonable prices.

food.name: Alley Cafe
food.tip: Arrive early morning for peaceful ambiance before tourist crowds descend on the Forbidden City area. Their hand-drip coffee pairs well with the quiet hutong atmosphere.

food.name: Imperial Palace Jiaolou Cafe
food.tip: Visit early morning to avoid crowds and snap photos of your coffee against the turret backdrop. The palace wall latte art is Instagram gold.

food.name: Pangmei Noodle Restaurant
food.tip: Arrive before noon to avoid the lunch rush. Try their hand-pulled noodles with zhajiangmian sauce—a local favorite that showcases authentic Beijing flavors.

food.name: Metal Hands
food.tip: Order their signature hand-drip coffee prepared by skilled baristas. Arrives mid-afternoon to avoid crowds and enjoy the industrial-chic atmosphere. WiFi and power outlets available for remote workers.

food.name: Soloist Coffee Co.
food.tip: Best for solo work sessions with quality single-origin beans. Arrive early afternoon for quieter atmosphere and fresh pastries. WiFi and power outlets available throughout.

food.name: Cafe Zarah
food.tip: Arrive early on weekends to snag a spot on the cozy outdoor terrace. Their homemade cakes pair perfectly with a strong cappuccino for a relaxed afternoon.

food.name: Tiantan Restaurant
food.tip: Located near Temple of Heaven, ideal for lunch after morning temple visits. Try their Beijing-style dishes like zhajiangmian or traditional palace cuisine at reasonable prices.

food.name: The Horizon
food.tip: Request a window seat for panoramic views of the Temple of Heaven area. Visit during sunset for the best atmosphere and lighting for photos.

food.name: Berrybeans
food.tip: Try their signature berry-infused lattes. Quieter during weekday mornings, making it ideal for remote work. Avoid peak lunch hours when Temple of Heaven tourists crowd in.

food.name: Tian Roast Coffee Co.
food.tip: Visit after exploring Temple of Heaven for a quality hand-drip coffee. Try their single-origin beans roasted in-house. Quieter on weekday mornings.

food.name: Xin Rong Ji
food.tip: Reserve ahead for their signature yellow croaker fish. Request a private room for the full experience. Lunch service offers better availability than dinner.

food.name: Fusan
food.tip: Visit in the afternoon after exploring Summer Palace for a quiet retreat. Try their specialty coffee blends and house-made pastries. Weekdays are less crowded than weekends.

food.name: Hutong Restaurant
food.tip: Located in the atmospheric hutong area near Drum Tower. Best visited during lunch for quieter ambiance. Ask staff for daily specials featuring seasonal Beijing ingredients.

food.name: Zongbu Hutong Zhaimencai
food.tip: Traditional Buddhist vegetarian cuisine served in a historic hutong setting. Try the signature mock meat dishes that showcase centuries-old temple cooking techniques. Reservations recommended for dinner.

food.name: Alba Cafe
food.tip: Tucked in a quiet hutong near the Drum Tower, this spot is perfect for afternoon coffee away from tourist crowds. Try their house blend with pastries.

food.name: Shengyongxing Roasted Duck Restaurant Sanlitun Branch
food.chinese: 盛永兴烤鸭店三里屯店
food.tip: Order the signature crispy roasted duck with traditional pancakes and sweet bean sauce. Arrive before 6pm to avoid peak dinner crowds and ensure duck availability.

food.name: Dadong Roast Duck Restaurant
food.tip: Order the signature lean roast duck with crispy skin and less fat. Reserve ahead for window seats. The duck is carved tableside—ask for extra pancakes and scallions.

food.name: Xingyuan Restaurant
food.tip: Popular neighborhood spot near Beijing Zoo. Arrive before noon to avoid lunch rush. Try their home-style Beijing dishes which locals frequent for reliable quality.

food.name: Kaorouji
food.tip: Order the signature roast lamb and sesame buns. Arrive early for dinner as this historic spot fills up quickly, especially on weekends.

food.name: Jubaoyuan Hot Pot Restaurant
food.tip: Order the hand-cut mutton, their specialty. Arrive before 6pm on weekends to avoid long waits. The sesame dipping sauce is essential.

hotels.name: The Peninsula Beijing
hotels.tip: The gold standard for Forbidden City access — on Jinyu Alley, 5-min walk to Tiananmen, Forbidden City, Wangfujing. Legendary Peninsula service consistency. Art Deco elegance. The rooftop bar has one of Beijing's best city views. Worth the splurge for first-timers who want the best central location.

hotels.name: Waldorf Astoria Beijing
hotels.tip: Art Deco luxury in a historic hutong courtyard near Wangfujing. Unique combination of hutong character and 5-star amenities. Walk to Forbidden City, Jingshan Park and Nanluoguxiang. Hilton Diamond benefits. Popular with couples and history-focused travellers.

hotels.name: Park Plaza Beijing Wangfujing
hotels.tip: Best mid-range in the Wangfujing area — IHG reliability, 4-star comfort, excellent location. Walk to Forbidden City in 15 minutes. Positive reviews for spacious rooms and good breakfast. Metro line 1 at the door. English-speaking staff praised consistently.

hotels.name: The Orchid Beijing (Hutong Boutique)
hotels.tip: The most celebrated boutique hutong hotel in Beijing — carefully restored courtyard, heated rooms (rare in hutongs), outstanding brunch. Walk to Drum Tower, Nanluoguxiang, Houhai Lake. Only a dozen rooms so book months ahead. The authentic Beijing experience done properly.

hotels.name: Holiday Inn Express Beijing Dongzhimen
hotels.chinese: 北京东直门智选假日酒店
hotels.tip: Best budget hotel in central Beijing — Dongzhimen location means Airport Express terminus 5 min away and Sanlitun 10 min walk. Free breakfast included. Reliable IHG budget brand. English-speaking staff. Praised for helpful service. Best value-for-location in Beijing.

beijing.recommended_base_reason: Dongcheng gives walkable access to Forbidden City, Hutongs and Drum Tower

kids_attractions.name: Universal Studios Beijing
kids_attractions.tip: World's largest Universal Studios — Harry Potter, Transformers, Minions, Kung Fu Panda, Jurassic World. Buy Express Pass for popular rides. Harry Potter's Forbidden Journey and the Kung Fu Panda ride are the crowd favourites. Avoid Chinese public holidays (May Day, National Day, Spring Festival) — queues become extreme. Book via Trip.com for discounts. Kids under 1.2m get discounted entry.

kids_attractions.name: Beijing Zoo + Aquarium
kids_attractions.chinese: 北京动物园 & 海洋馆
kids_attractions.tip: Beijing's oldest zoo with 5,000+ animals including giant pandas (star attraction). The attached Aquarium (¥120 separate ticket) has an 80m underwater tunnel, beluga whale viewing, dolphin shows and mermaid performances. Do the aquarium first (less walking) then the pandas. Kids under 1.2m enter both free.

kids_attractions.name: China Science & Technology Museum
kids_attractions.tip: 300+ interactive exhibits across science, space, robotics and technology. The giant IMAX dome theatre shows space and nature films (¥20 extra). Children can control robots, fly a virtual aircraft and explore the history of Chinese inventions. Best for ages 6–14. Book ahead during school holidays.

kids_attractions.name: Beijing Natural History Museum
kids_attractions.tip: Outstanding dinosaur collection — complete T-Rex and Brachiosaurus skeletons. The Hall of Ancient Life traces evolution with remarkable fossils. The Human Body Science exhibit is interactive and educational. Free but must book via WeChat. Hugely popular with school groups — avoid weekday mornings during term time.

kids_attractions.name: Watercube Indoor Waterpark
kids_attractions.tip: Inside the iconic 2008 Olympics Water Cube — China's first fully themed indoor waterpark. 13 water slides, wave pool, lazy river and a separate toddler splash zone. The bubble-architecture exterior is the most photographed building in Beijing. Book tickets online. Open year-round regardless of weather.

kids_attractions.name: Happy Valley Beijing
kids_attractions.tip: Chinese theme park with 100+ rides across 7 themed zones. Better for thrillseeker families with older kids (8+) than Universal. The Wooden Roller Coaster is one of China's most beloved. Much cheaper than Universal. Avoid peak holidays. Annual passes offer excellent value for Beijing residents.

hangzhou.name: Hangzhou
hangzhou.tagline: Heaven on Earth — China's Most Romantic City

attractions.name: West Lake (Xi Hu)
attractions.tip: UNESCO World Heritage Site. Walk the Su Causeway (苏堤) for cross-lake views — 2km from end to end. Rent a bicycle (¥5/hr) to circle the full lake (15km). Early morning mist is magical. The ¥70 rowboat ferry is the best way to see 三潭印月 (Three Pools).
attractions.description: China's most celebrated body of water — the 10 classical views of West Lake that Chinese painters have been reproducing for 1,000 years are all real: mist on the water at dawn, lotus flowers in summer, snow-covered pagodas in winter. Su Dongpo called it the most beautiful place on earth.

attractions.name: Lingyin Temple + Feilai Peak
attractions.tip: Founded 1,700 years ago — one of China's most important Buddhist temples. Go before 8am to see monks chanting morning prayers. The giant laughing Buddha at Feilai Peak is the most famous. The 470 stone Buddha carvings along the stream path span 1,000 years of art.
attractions.description: One of China's ten greatest Buddhist temples — founded in 328 AD in a valley of ancient camphor trees, its main hall houses a 24-metre gilded camphor wood Buddha. The carved grottoes of the adjacent Feilai Peak contain 345 sculptures dating from the 10th-14th centuries.

attractions.name: Dragon Well Tea Village
attractions.tip: China's most famous tea village surrounded by terraced gardens. Sit with a local farmer and drink Dragon Well tea while watching the mountains. Buy directly from farmers (¥80–300/100g) not from tourist shops. Spring first harvest (明前, before April 5th) is the most prized.
attractions.description: The source of China's most celebrated green tea — Longjing (Dragon Well) tea is grown on hillside terraces surrounding a village that has been producing the same variety since the Tang dynasty. Visit in April during the Qingming harvest.
attractions.photo_query: longjing tea dragon well hangzhou village
attractions.photo_attribution: Inquiring About Tea at Dragon Well

attractions.name: Broken Bridge
attractions.tip: Most romantic bridge in China from the White Snake legend — despite the name, it's not broken. Best in snow (rare in Hangzhou), or in spring when weeping willows line the walkway. The bridge connects White Causeway to the main shore.
attractions.description: The most famous bridge on West Lake — a Han dynasty arched stone bridge that appears 'broken' in winter when snow melts faster at the heated bridge joints, creating a gap in the white surface. The setting for one of China's most beloved folk tales about a white snake spirit.
attractions.photo_query: broken bridge west lake hangzhou snow

attractions.name: Hefang Street
attractions.tip: Ming dynasty commercial street. Home to Hu Qingyu Tang pharmacy (胡庆余堂, 1874) — the most beautiful traditional Chinese medicine pharmacy in China, free to enter. Try the street snacks: Ding Sheng Gao (定胜糕), Cong Bao Gui (葱包桧) and cold chicken. Lanterns at night are gorgeous.
attractions.description: Hangzhou's most authentic historical commercial street — the Song dynasty Qinghefang area where traditional medicine shops and craft workshops occupy buildings from the Republic era. The osmanthus wine and West Lake fish in vinegar sauce available here are the definitive Hangzhou food experiences.

attractions.name: Leifeng Pagoda
attractions.tip: Take the elevator to the golden top for the best view of the whole West Lake and mountains. The glass floors inside reveal the Song dynasty original brick pagoda ruins below your feet. The best sunset view in Hangzhou — stay for golden hour over the lake.
attractions.description: A Song dynasty pagoda rebuilt on its original site — standing on the southern shore of West Lake, its amber form reflected perfectly in the still water is the most reproduced image of Hangzhou. Climb to the top for the best view of the lake's full extent at sunset.

attractions.name: Xixi National Wetland Park
attractions.tip: China's only urban national wetland park — 11km² of waterways and lotus ponds. Take the included boat tour first (45 mins), then explore the walking trails. Best in late spring (lotus flowers) or autumn (reeds turn gold). The hot air balloon ride over the wetlands (¥300) is unforgettable.
attractions.description: China's first and only urban wetland national park — 11.5km² of channels, lakes and islands covered in plum, osmanthus and tangerine orchards that have been farmed by fishermen for 2,000 years. The dragon boat festival celebration here is the most atmospheric in China.
attractions.photo_query: xixi wetland park hangzhou water nature

attractions.name: Wuzhen Water Town (Day Trip)
attractions.tip: One of the best-preserved ancient water towns in China — 1.5hrs from Hangzhou by bus. The West Scenic Zone (西栅) has the most atmospheric evening views. Ancient Ming/Qing architecture, stone bridges and gondola rides. Book ahead in Golden Week — sell out weeks in advance.
attractions.description: A must-visit historic-district in Hangzhou that showcases the best of local culture and history. Ask your hotel concierge for the best time to visit and any advance booking requirements.

attractions.name: Baochu Pagoda & Precious Stone Hill
attractions.tip: Free hike with panoramic views of the whole West Lake — far less visited than other viewpoints. The 1,000-year-old slender pagoda at the top. Best at sunrise (arrive by 5:45am in summer). The hillside path is lined with pomegranate trees that flower red in summer.
attractions.description: A slender Song dynasty pagoda on a rocky ridge overlooking West Lake — its elegant silhouette against the misty lake and surrounding hills has been the defining element of Hangzhou's skyline for 1,000 years. The hill walk up to the pagoda through bamboo forest is as beautiful as the destination.
attractions.photo_query: baochu pagoda hangzhou west lake hill

attractions.name: Longjing Tea Plantation Trail
attractions.tip: A free walking trail through the actual Dragon Well tea gardens — rows of tea bushes as far as you can see. Best in spring when pickers are harvesting and the air smells of fresh green tea. Start from the top of the village and walk down. Completely free.
attractions.description: A must-visit nature in Hangzhou that showcases the best of local culture and history. Ask your hotel concierge for the best time to visit and any advance booking requirements.

attractions.name: Zhongshan Road Night Market
attractions.tip: The 1,400m Southern Song Imperial Street by night — Hangzhou's most atmospheric night market. Traditional snack stalls, heritage Chinese medicine shops and lanterns overhead. Best after 6pm. The Red Carp Pool (金鱼池) at the end has street performers most evenings.
attractions.description: A must-visit local-hangout in Hangzhou that showcases the best of local culture and history. Ask your hotel concierge for the best time to visit and any advance booking requirements.

attractions.name: Yue Fei Temple
attractions.tip: Memorial temple for Song dynasty general Yue Fei — China's most revered patriot. The iron statues of the four traitors who caused his death are kneeling in front of his tomb, eternally shamed. Moving and historically significant. Combine with a walk along the northern lakeshore.
attractions.description: The memorial temple to China's most beloved military hero — General Yue Fei, wrongly executed in 1142AD, is commemorated with a magnificent Song dynasty temple complex on the northern bank of West Lake. The iron statues of his betrayers kneeling in shame at the tomb entrance are particularly powerful.
attractions.photo_query: yue fei temple hangzhou west lake

attractions.name: Faxitang Flower Market
attractions.tip: The 'prayer temple' that's gone viral for its beautiful yellow walls and flower offerings. Dramatically less crowded than Lingyin Temple. Locals believe it's especially efficacious for romantic relationships — full of couples lighting incense. Combine with nearby Dragon Well Village.
attractions.description: A must-visit temple in Hangzhou that showcases the best of local culture and history. Ask your hotel concierge for the best time to visit and any advance booking requirements.
attractions.photo_attribution: Hefang Street Art And Craft Gift Store

attractions.name: Hangzhou Museum & Qinghefang Area
attractions.tip: Excellent free museum with the famous 战国水晶杯 (Warring States crystal cup, 2,500 years old) and Liangzhu jade pieces. Must book in advance via WeChat. The surrounding Qinghefang historic area has excellent street food nearby. Closed Mondays.
attractions.description: The commercial and cultural heart of Hangzhou's historic district — the Qinghefang pedestrian area combines traditional medicine shops, craft workshops and street food vendors in a beautifully restored Song-dynasty streetscape adjacent to West Lake.

attractions.name: Qiandao Lake (Thousand Island Lake)
attractions.tip: 1,078 islands in an enormous freshwater reservoir — one of China's most photographed landscapes. About 2hrs from Hangzhou by bus. The boat tours between islands are the main experience. The water is crystal clear — swimming in summer is allowed in designated areas.
attractions.description: An artificial lake created in 1959 containing 1,078 forested islands — the water is so clean it is piped to Shanghai as drinking water. The island-hopping boat tours and the dramatic vista of forested hills emerging from turquoise water are extraordinary.

attractions.name: Hangzhou Botanical Garden
attractions.tip: Enter through the north gate near Yuquan Campus to start at the stunning bamboo garden section, then work your way south. Weekday mornings offer the quietest experience before tour groups arrive after 10am.
attractions.description: This sprawling botanical garden nestled against West Lake's western hills showcases themed zones from bamboo groves to medicinal herb collections across both manicured pleasure grounds and working research areas. Come for peaceful walks through diverse plant landscapes where verdant scenery feels worlds away from the city, yet remains surprisingly accessible.

attractions.name: West Lake Scenic Resort
attractions.tip: Rent a bicycle at any of the stations along the lake perimeter to cover more ground—the flat 15km loop is perfect for exploring hidden viewpoints locals favor at dawn.
attractions.description: This UNESCO-listed lake has inspired Chinese poets and artists for over a thousand years with its willow-lined causeways, arched bridges, and misty mountain backdrop. Stroll the scenic pathways to discover pagodas, lotus ponds, and pavilions that embody classical Chinese landscape aesthetics.

attractions.name: Three Pools Mirroring the Moon
attractions.tip: Visit during Mid-Autumn Festival when candles are lit inside the pagodas' openings, creating glowing moon reflections on the water. Arrive by ferry just before sunset to catch golden hour and stay for the lighting ceremony.
attractions.description: Three ancient stone pagodas rise from the glassy waters of West Lake, their hollow towers creating the illusion of multiple moons reflected on the water during moonlit nights. This iconic scene graces the back of China's 1 yuan note, making it the country's most photographed lakeside landmark.

attractions.name: Baoshi Mountain
attractions.tip: Start your hike from the Yellow Dragon Cave entrance on the west side instead of the crowded lakefront trailhead—you'll encounter fewer tourists and discover small temples hidden among the rocks along the way.
attractions.description: This forested hill rises directly above West Lake's northern shore, offering winding stone paths through bamboo groves and weathered pavilions. Climb to the summit for sweeping panoramas of the lake, city skyline, and Leifeng Pagoda across the water.

attractions.name: Longjing Mountain Park
attractions.tip: Visit early morning to watch tea farmers hand-picking leaves in the terraces. The lower trails near Longjing Village offer free tea tastings at family-run farms where prices are more honest than tourist shops.
attractions.description: This hillside park winds through terraced tea plantations where Hangzhou's legendary Longjing Dragon Well tea has been cultivated for centuries. Come for peaceful walks among emerald tea bushes, traditional tea villages, and panoramic views over West Lake below.

attractions.name: Inquiring About Tea at Dragon Well
attractions.tip: Visit in early April before Qingming Festival when pre-rain tea leaves fetch premium prices and taste sweetest. Locals buy directly from family-run farms along the village paths for better prices than touristy shops.
attractions.description: Terraced tea plantations cascade down hillsides in Longjing Village, where generations have cultivated China's most celebrated green tea. Visit traditional tea houses to witness hand-roasting techniques and sample fresh Dragon Well tea amid fragrant fields.

attractions.name: West Lake Hangzhou Benshan Longjing Tea Museum
attractions.chinese: 西湖杭州本山龙井茶叶博物馆
attractions.tip: Visit during late March to early April to witness tea farmers hand-picking the prized pre-Qingming leaves. Ask staff to demonstrate the eight-motion hand-roasting technique in the traditional wok.
attractions.description: This specialized museum nestled in Longjing village showcases the complete heritage of authentic Dragon Well tea cultivation and processing. Experience the artistry behind China's most famous green tea through traditional tools, demonstrations, and the fragrant atmosphere of surrounding tea plantations.

attractions.name: Meijiawu Tea Village
attractions.tip: Visit small teahouses on the upper village paths rather than tourist-packed main street establishments. Ask to see tea being hand-roasted in woks—many families still do this the traditional way in spring.
attractions.description: This peaceful tea-farming village nestled in verdant hills is where generations of families have cultivated Longjing Dragon Well tea. Walk through terraced plantations, watch traditional tea processing, and sip fresh brews at family-run teahouses with mountain views.

attractions.name: Lingyin Temple
attractions.tip: Visit Feilai Feng grottoes across from the temple entrance first—hundreds of ancient Buddhist carvings are included in your ticket but often missed by visitors who head straight to the main halls.
attractions.description: Hangzhou's oldest Buddhist temple emerges from misty forests, its prayer halls filled with sandalwood smoke and chanting monks since 328 CE. The towering Hall of the Great Hero houses an 20-meter gilded Buddha, while ancient ginkgo trees frame courtyards where devotees light incense at dawn.

attractions.name: Song Dynasty Town
attractions.tip: Book tickets online in advance for significant discounts on the evening show, which is the main attraction. Arrive by 5pm to explore the park before the 7pm performance and avoid paying just for the show without experiencing the grounds.
attractions.description: This sprawling cultural theme park recreates life during China's Song Dynasty with traditional architecture, costumed performers, and atmospheric streets. The highlight is the spectacular Song Dynasty Show, a Vegas-caliber theatrical production featuring acrobatics, dance, and special effects.

attractions.name: Jixian Pavilion
attractions.tip: Visit during late afternoon when the setting sun casts golden light across the canal waters, creating ideal conditions for photography without the midday crowds.
attractions.description: This elegant waterside pavilion stands along the historic Grand Canal, offering a peaceful retreat with traditional architecture and reflective water views. It serves as a perfect vantage point to watch boats glide past while soaking in centuries of canal-side heritage.

attractions.name: Qinghefang Ancient Street
attractions.tip: Visit the Hu Qing Yu Tang Chinese Medicine Museum on the street for free tea tastings and to see how traditional remedies are made using centuries-old techniques.
attractions.description: This bustling pedestrian street near West Lake recreates old Hangzhou with Qing Dynasty architecture, traditional shops, and street food vendors. Come for authentic local snacks, traditional medicine shops, and silk stores that have traded here for centuries.

food.name: West Lake Vinegar Fish
food.where: Lou Wai Lou (楼外楼) — lakeside since 1848
food.tip: Hangzhou's most iconic dish — whole grass carp in sweet vinegar glaze. Must be cooked in under 4 minutes to preserve delicate texture. Lou Wai Lou has the most scenic setting overlooking Gu Shan island. Book lakeside tables ahead.

food.name: Dragon Well Shrimp
food.where: Zhiwei Guan (知味观) — Hangzhou's oldest restaurant group
food.tip: River shrimp stir-fried with fresh Dragon Well tea leaves — a uniquely Hangzhou dish. The tea gives it a subtle grassy fragrance. Only authentic in spring (April) with fresh first-harvest leaves. The rest of the year uses dried leaves which are less aromatic.

food.name: Dongpo Pork
food.where: Tianxiang Lou (天香楼) or Zhiwei Guan (知味观)
food.tip: Braised pork belly named after poet Su Dongpo who invented it here in the 11th century. Melt-in-your-mouth layered belly slow-braised in Shaoxing wine and soy. The fat becomes perfectly jiggly — not greasy at all. Served in individual clay pots.

food.name: Ding Sheng Gao (Victory Cake)
food.where: Hefang Street stalls and traditional pastry shops
food.tip: Pink cloud-shaped steamed rice cakes with sweet red bean filling — a Song dynasty snack that generals gave soldiers before battle for good luck (定胜 = 'certain victory'). Soft, lightly sweet, completely moreish. Hangzhou's most photogenic street snack.

food.name: Xia Bao Shan Mian (Shrimp Noodles)
food.where: Kuiyuan Guan (奎元馆) — Hangzhou's oldest noodle restaurant, est. 1867
food.tip: Hangzhou's signature noodle — river shrimp and crispy fried eel on thick noodles in dark soy broth. The eel is flash-fried until golden and stays crispy even in soup. Kuiyuan Guan has been serving this since 1867. Very satisfying combination of textures.

food.name: Longjing Tea & Snacks
food.where: Tea houses in Dragon Well Village or West Lake waterfront
food.tip: China's most prized green tea. Sit in a local farmhouse tea room, watch the hills and sip. First harvest (明前龙井 before April 5th) is most prized. The tea has a chestnut aroma completely different from any Longjing you've had outside China.

food.name: Cong Bao Gui (Green Onion Bake)
food.where: Hefang Street stalls near Qinghefang
food.tip: Hangzhou's unique street food — a crispy green onion and plain fried dough stick pressed flat on a griddle and glazed with sweet and spicy sauce. Named after Yue Fei's traitor Qin Gui — pressing the dough stick is symbolic revenge. ¥6 and utterly satisfying.

food.name: West Lake Su Causeway Cafes
food.must_order: Longjing green tea latte, lotus seed cake
food.tip: Several cafes along the Su Causeway with direct West Lake views. Morning mist over the lake in spring and autumn is magical — some of China's most photographed scenery. The combination of weeping willows, peach blossoms (March–April) and a West Lake view from your table is exceptional.

food.name: Longjing Village Tea Farms
food.near: Longjing Village, 20 min from West Lake by bus
food.must_order: Freshly picked and roasted Longjing green tea, tea eggs
food.tip: Visit the actual tea farms where China's most famous green tea is grown. Farmers sell direct — 3x cheaper than city shops and infinitely fresher. Watch the tea being pan-roasted right in front of you. The terraced tea fields with misty mountains behind are stunning in spring (April is harvest season). Sit at a farm table and taste 5 different grades.

food.name: Hefang Street (河坊街) Snacks
food.near: Hefang Street, Shangcheng District, 15 min from West Lake
food.must_order: West Lake vinegar fish (醋鱼), dongpo pork, osmanthus glutinous rice
food.tip: Hangzhou's historic food street — osmanthus wine, West Lake lotus root, fried spring rolls. The West Lake vinegar fish (醋鱼) is the signature Hangzhou dish — mild, sweet, fragrant with vinegar. Louwailou restaurant (over 170 years old) on the lake is where you should eat it if budget allows. ¥120–160/dish.

food.name: Canal-side Cafes (Grand Canal)
food.must_order: Coffee with canal view, local pastries
food.tip: The Grand Canal area (especially Gongchen Bridge area) has a cluster of design cafes overlooking the ancient waterway. Much less crowded than West Lake. UNESCO Grand Canal heritage scenery with wooden boats, old bridges and weeping willows. Excellent evening photography location.

food.name: Zhiweiguan (知味观) Restaurant
food.must_order: Xiao Long Bao, Dongpo Pork, West Lake fish in sweet-sour sauce
food.tip: Hangzhou's most famous and beloved traditional restaurant — over 100 years old. Dongpo pork here (named after the poet Su Dongpo who invented it in Hangzhou) is the benchmark. Rich, melt-in-mouth, perfectly braised. The restaurant is a Chinese heritage brand. Go for dinner — lunch gets very crowded with tour groups.

food.name: West Lake Hangzhou Scenic Area Chinese Food Restaurant
food.chinese: 西湖杭州风景区中餐厅
food.tip: Request a table with West Lake views for the best dining experience. Try local Hangzhou specialties like West Lake vinegar fish or Dongpo pork.

food.name: Xihu Tiandi
food.tip: Visit during late afternoon for prime West Lake views from the outdoor terrace. The coffee and pastries are overpriced but worth it for the location and ambiance.

food.name: Bean's Cafe
food.tip: Perfect West Lake pit stop for quality coffee. Visit during weekday mornings to avoid crowds and snag a window seat overlooking the scenic area.

food.name: Longjing Teahouse
food.tip: Visit in late morning to watch traditional tea ceremonies. Order the premium Longjing tea picked before Qingming Festival for the most delicate flavor.

food.name: Qiannong Coffee
food.tip: Visit in late morning to pair specialty coffee with views of nearby Longjing tea terraces. Try their local tea-infused coffee for a unique Hangzhou twist.

food.name: Blue Coffee
food.tip: Perfectly positioned near Longjing tea fields for a post-hike coffee break. Try their signature cold brew paired with local pastries while enjoying views of the surrounding tea plantations.

food.name: Hangzhou Cuisine Restaurant First
food.tip: Order the West Lake vinegar fish and Dongpo pork, signature Hangzhou dishes. Arrive early during lunch for the freshest ingredients and quieter atmosphere.

food.name: i17 Cafe
food.tip: A hidden gem near the historic Grand Canal. Arrive early on weekends to secure a window seat with canal views. Their specialty coffee blends are locally roasted.

food.name: Costa Coffee
food.tip: Try the signature flat white with an almond croissant. Visit during weekday afternoons to avoid the morning rush and enjoy quieter seating by the canal.

food.name: Hangzhou Restaurant
food.tip: Arrive before 11:30am for lunch to avoid crowds. Order the signature West Lake fish in vinegar sauce and Dongpo pork for authentic Hangzhou flavors.

food.name: C.straits Cafe
food.tip: With a lower rating, this spot may be hit-or-miss. Visit during off-peak hours for better service and check recent reviews before going.

food.name: Greybox Coffee
food.tip: A specialty coffee spot perfect for a quiet afternoon work session. Try their hand-pour options and pair with light pastries for the best experience.

food.name: Half Day
food.tip: Perfect for a slow morning escape. Arrive early on weekends to snag a window seat and enjoy their hand-drip coffee in a relaxed atmosphere.

hotels.name: Four Seasons Hangzhou at West Lake
hotels.tip: The finest hotel at West Lake — pavilion-style villas set in a classical Chinese garden right on the lakefront. Private boat pier, excellent spa. Forbes Five-Star. Walking distance to Leifeng Pagoda and Su Causeway. One of the most beautiful hotel settings in China.

hotels.name: Banyan Tree Hangzhou
hotels.tip: Banyan Tree luxury on West Lake — famous spa, stilted villas over the water, private lake access. More intimate than Four Seasons. Exceptional for couples and honeymoon travellers. Both properties are next to each other — Four Seasons slightly edges for families.

hotels.name: Hyatt Regency Hangzhou
hotels.tip: Best value 5-star near West Lake — Hyatt reliability at lower prices than Four Seasons or Banyan Tree. Walk to lake in 10 minutes. Good pool, breakfast praised. English-friendly staff. Solid choice for families and business travellers wanting West Lake access without luxury prices.

hotels.name: Radisson Blu Hangzhou
hotels.tip: Well-located 4-star near the Grand Canal — slightly away from West Lake but excellent value. Praised for spacious rooms and breakfast variety. Good metro access. A strong mid-range choice if West Lake hotels are full or over budget.

hotels.name: James Joyce Coffetel Hangzhou Xihu
hotels.chinese: 杭州西湖詹姆斯乔伊斯文化酒店
